title: Stack and Store Lap Desks
summary: Open-sided lap desks that nest into a stack when the crew's done — five cuts from a 1x12 and a 1x4 each, with jigsawed grab handles. Build one per kid.
description:
The other lap desk in this catalog folds; this one STACKS. Each desk is a three-sided box — 1x12 sides, 1x4 rails across the back and front underside, 1x12 top — open at the front so legs slide under, with hand-hole handles jigsawed into the top. Built to the same footprint, they nest into a tidy tower by the couch, which is the entire product: one desk per kid, one stack per living room, zero arguments about whose is whose (label them; there will still be arguments).

Five cuts per desk. The 1x4s screw to the 1x12 sides, the top lands with even overhangs, glue everywhere, fifteen screws. The handles are the only tool trick: drill a starter hole at each end of the marked handle slot, connect the holes with the jigsaw, and sand the opening until it's kind to small fingers — the same technique as every toolbox tote, at homework scale.

Because laps are soft and kids drag these by the handles, round every edge and corner past 'smooth' to 'friendly.' Finish with something wipeable — these will meet markers, cereal, and glitter glue, likely simultaneously. A different paint color per kid turns the stack into a rainbow and settles ownership disputes at a glance.

tools.note: The grab-handle slots in the top.
Screws, plus the handle starter holes.
Round everything to lap-grade. Handles especially.

materials.name: 1x12, 4 ft
materials.note: Sides and top for one desk.

materials.name: Paint and wipeable topcoat
materials.note: A different color per kid ends the whose-is-whose debate.

cutList.note: Even overhang at each end — it's also the stacking registration.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two sides at 11-1/4" and one top at 25" from the 1x12. Cut two rails at 22-1/2" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 4 ft 1x12 and one 4 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Rails to sides
steps.body: Screw and glue the two 1x4 rails across the 1x12 sides per the diagram — one high at the back, one low at the front — two screws per joint. Keep the assembly square; the desks only stack straight if each box is.

steps.title: Top on
steps.body: Glue the top edges of the sides and back rail, set the top with even end overhangs, and screw down into the sides and the back 1x4. If you're building the fleet, use the first desk as the pattern for the rest — matching footprints are what make the stack.

steps.title: Handles, sand, paint
steps.body:
Mark each handle slot, drill a starter hole at both ends, connect them with the jigsaw, and sand the opening genuinely smooth — fingers wrap this edge every single carry.

Round all the outer corners, paint each desk its owner's color, topcoat with something scrubbable, and stack the finished fleet by the couch. Homework has no excuses left; that part's not the desk's fault.

images.alt: Stack of nesting wooden lap desks in different colors
